content.text: Why AI Incidents Require a Distinct Crisis Comms Approach

AI incidents differ from standard software incidents in two ways that affect communications strategy. First, the causal chain is less transparent: when a bug produces wrong output, the mechanism is usually traceable; when a language model hallucinates a clause, the failure is probabilistic and not a defect in the conventional sense. This makes explaining what happened harder. Second, the audience anxiety is higher. Employees and the public have pre-existing beliefs about AI reliability that a single visible failure can confirm, regardless of the base rate. A crisis communications plan for AI incidents must account for both.

The most damaging response to an AI incident is not a slow response. It is an incomplete response that attributes the failure to 'the AI' and leaves open the question of what process failed and who is accountable for fixing it.
Incident Severity Classification

attrs.alt: AI Incident Crisis Comms -- response timeline and severity bands

content.text: A classification system determines who is notified, how fast, and through what channels. Without one, every incident produces either over-reaction (treating a near-miss as a public crisis) or under-reaction (treating a client-facing error as a minor internal note). Three severity levels cover the range:
Severity
Definition
Example
Comms Deadline
Audience
P1
External harm: client, user, or regulatory exposure
Hallucinated contract clause acted upon by client; customer data included in AI output
External statement within 4h; internal within 2h
CEO, Legal, Comms, affected clients, regulator if required
P2
Internal quality failure: no external harm
AI extraction error caught before delivery; wrong suggestion shipped to staging
Internal notice within 8h
Programme Manager, team lead, impacted team
P3
Near-miss: failure caught before any impact
Analyst identified hallucination during routine review; eval test caught regression
Team log entry + weekly review
Programme Manager only
Incident Model and Escalation Logic

The incident model ties together detection, classification, deadline tracking, and escalation checks. The escalation check is critical: a P3 near-miss that had an external exposure path (the hallucination reached a client draft that was then caught before sending) is a P2, not a P3.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class IncidentSeverity(Enum):
    P1 = "p1"   # external harm or regulatory exposure
    P2 = "p2"   # internal quality failure, no external harm
    P3 = "p3"   # near-miss, caught before impact


class IncidentStatus(Enum):
    OPEN = "open"
    CONTAINED = "contained"
    ROOT_CAUSE_IDENTIFIED = "root_cause_identified"
    FIXED = "fixed"
    CLOSED = "closed"


@dataclass
class AIIncident:
    incident_id: str
    severity: IncidentSeverity
    detected_at: datetime
    tool_name: str
    affected_function: str
    what_happened: str
    affected_outputs: list[str]
    external_exposure: bool
    status: IncidentStatus = IncidentStatus.OPEN
    root_cause: Optional[str] = None
    process_fix: Optional[str] = None
    comms_owner: str = "Programme Manager"
    legal_notified: bool = False
    external_statement_issued: bool = False

    def initial_comms_deadline(self) -> datetime:
        deadlines = {
            IncidentSeverity.P1: timedelta(hours=2),
            IncidentSeverity.P2: timedelta(hours=8),
            IncidentSeverity.P3: timedelta(days=7),
        }
        return self.detected_at + deadlines[self.severity]

    def escalation_check(self) -> Optional[str]:
        if self.severity == IncidentSeverity.P3 and self.external_exposure:
            return "Escalate to P2: near-miss had external exposure path"
        if self.severity == IncidentSeverity.P2 and self.external_exposure:
            return "Escalate to P1: internal failure has external-facing component"
        return None

    def validate_before_close(self) -> list[str]:
        issues = []
        if not self.root_cause:
            issues.append("Root cause not documented -- incident cannot be closed")
        if not self.process_fix:
            issues.append("No process fix recorded -- same failure can recur")
        if self.severity == IncidentSeverity.P1 and not self.external_statement_issued:
            issues.append("P1 incident requires external statement before close")
        if self.severity in (IncidentSeverity.P1, IncidentSeverity.P2) and not self.legal_notified:
            issues.append("Legal must be notified for P1/P2 before close")
        return issues


inc = AIIncident(
    incident_id="INC-2026-112",
    severity=IncidentSeverity.P2,
    detected_at=datetime(2026, 7, 1, 9, 14),
    tool_name="ContractSummariser v2.4",
    affected_function="Legal Services -- Contracts",
    what_happened=(
        "AI summariser produced an incorrect liability cap figure (2M instead of 200k) "
        "on three contracts processed between 08:00 and 09:10. Error detected during "
        "analyst review before any client delivery."
    ),
    affected_outputs=["contract-2026-441", "contract-2026-442", "contract-2026-443"],
    external_exposure=False,
)

deadline = inc.initial_comms_deadline()
escalation = inc.escalation_check()
print(f"Incident: {inc.incident_id} [{inc.severity.value.upper()}]")
print(f"Initial comms deadline: {deadline.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')}")
print(f"Escalation required: {escalation or 'None'}")
Authoring the Initial Internal Notice

The initial internal notice must be sent within the time bound for the severity level. Its purpose is not to explain the root cause (not yet known) but to confirm that leadership is aware, has contained the issue, and is investigating. A notice that claims to know the root cause before the investigation is complete creates a second communications problem when that claim turns out to be wrong.
What the Initial Notice Must Include
What happened in specific, factual terms. Not 'the AI produced an error' but 'the ContractSummariser extracted an incorrect liability figure on three contracts processed this morning.'
What has been done immediately: suspension of affected functionality, manual review of outputs, preservation of evidence.
What is not yet known. Explicitly stating what the investigation has not yet determined prevents speculation from filling the gap.
A single named owner with contact details. Not a team inbox.
When the next update will be sent. A specific time, not 'as soon as we know more.'
What the Initial Notice Must Not Include
A premature root cause.
'We believe this was caused by...' at hour 2 is almost always incomplete. State confirmed facts only.
Blame attribution.
Naming individuals in the initial notice creates a blame culture that suppresses future error reporting. Root cause is always a process finding.
Reassurance that overpromises.
'This will not happen again' before a fix exists destroys credibility when a similar incident occurs.
Speculation about external impact.
If external exposure is not yet confirmed, state that. Do not confirm or deny until the scope assessment is complete.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class CrisisCommsNotice:
    incident_id: str
    severity_label: str
    authored_at: datetime
    author_name: str
    what_happened: str
    immediate_actions: list[str]
    what_is_not_yet_known: list[str]
    single_point_of_contact: str
    next_update_at: datetime
    audience: str

    def render(self) -> str:
        actions = "\n".join(f"  * {a}" for a in self.immediate_actions)
        unknowns = "\n".join(f"  * {u}" for u in self.what_is_not_yet_known)
        return (
            f"AI Incident Notice -- {self.incident_id} [{self.severity_label}]\n"
            f"Issued: {self.authored_at.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')} by {self.author_name}\n"
            f"Audience: {self.audience}\n\n"
            f"WHAT HAPPENED\n{self.what_happened}\n\n"
            f"IMMEDIATE ACTIONS TAKEN\n{actions}\n\n"
            f"WHAT WE DO NOT YET KNOW\n{unknowns}\n\n"
            f"SINGLE POINT OF CONTACT\n{self.single_point_of_contact}\n\n"
            f"NEXT UPDATE\n{self.next_update_at.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')}"
        )

    def completeness_check(self) -> list[str]:
        issues = []
        if not self.what_is_not_yet_known:
            issues.append("Missing uncertainty section -- omitting unknowns signals overconfidence")
        if "believe" in self.what_happened.lower() or "think" in self.what_happened.lower():
            issues.append("Avoid hedged factual claims -- state confirmed facts only")
        if not self.single_point_of_contact:
            issues.append("No single point of contact defined")
        return issues


notice = CrisisCommsNotice(
    incident_id="INC-2026-112",
    severity_label="P2",
    authored_at=datetime(2026, 7, 1, 10, 30),
    author_name="A. Okonkwo, Programme Manager",
    what_happened=(
        "The ContractSummariser tool produced an incorrect liability cap figure on three "
        "contracts processed between 08:00 and 09:10 this morning. The error was detected "
        "during standard analyst review. No documents have been delivered to clients "
        "with the incorrect figure."
    ),
    immediate_actions=[
        "ContractSummariser suspended for all users as of 09:45",
        "Three affected contracts quarantined pending manual re-extraction",
        "All contracts processed since 08:00 flagged for analyst review before delivery",
        "Evidence log and model output preserved for root cause analysis",
    ],
    what_is_not_yet_known=[
        "Root cause -- whether error is model-level, prompt-level, or data-input-level",
        "Whether any contracts outside the 08:00-09:10 window were affected",
        "When ContractSummariser will be reinstated",
    ],
    single_point_of_contact="A. Okonkwo -- a.okonkwo@company.com / ext. 4421",
    next_update_at=datetime(2026, 7, 1, 16, 0),
    audience="Legal Services team and Programme Steering Group",
)

print(notice.render())
issues = notice.completeness_check()
if issues:
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  WARNING: {issue}")
else:
    print("  Notice passes completeness check")
Root Cause Communication

The root cause communication is the most consequential document in an AI incident. It answers the question employees are most anxious about: 'Was this a one-off or does this mean the tool is fundamentally unreliable?' The answer must be specific enough to be credible, honest about the failure mechanism, and anchored to a concrete process change. Root cause for AI incidents almost always falls into one of four categories:
Root Cause Category
Example
Comms Implication
Known model limitation not reflected in safe-use guidance
Model documented as unreliable on contracts > 50 pages; this was not in analyst training
Admit the documentation existed and was not acted on; describe the process gap that allowed it
Prompt or context window failure
Contract fragment exceeded context limit; model completed with plausible but incorrect values
Explain the mechanism plainly; describe the input validation fix
Evaluation gap: failure mode not in test suite
Test suite did not include nested liability structures; error would have been caught
Describe what the eval suite now covers; acknowledge what else it may still miss
Human workflow bypass
Analyst under deadline pressure skipped validation step that would have caught the error
Do not name the individual; describe the process that allowed the bypass; describe the control that prevents it

Every root cause communication should end with: 'Here is what we changed in the process so this specific failure cannot recur in this way.' If you cannot state a specific process change, the investigation is not complete.
External Communications for P1 Incidents

When an AI incident has caused or plausibly caused harm to clients, customers, or third parties, external communication is required. The external statement is subject to legal review, may be visible to regulators and press, and must not contain operational detail that could create additional liability. The model and prompt architecture of the system are not disclosed in external statements.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class ExternalIncidentStatement:
    organisation_name: str
    incident_date: date
    what_clients_experienced: str
    immediate_remediation: str
    process_change: str
    contact_for_affected_clients: str
    statement_date: date
    legal_reviewed: bool = False
    comms_reviewed: bool = False

    def render(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"{self.organisation_name} -- Statement Regarding AI Processing Incident\n\n"
            f"On {self.incident_date.strftime('%d %B %Y')}, some documents produced by "
            f"{self.organisation_name} contained an error arising from an AI-assisted processing step.\n\n"
            f"What happened:\n{self.what_clients_experienced}\n\n"
            f"What we have done:\n{self.immediate_remediation}\n\n"
            f"What we have changed:\n{self.process_change}\n\n"
            f"If you have questions or believe you may be affected, please contact:\n"
            f"{self.contact_for_affected_clients}"
        )

    def pre_publish_check(self) -> list[str]:
        issues = []
        if not self.legal_reviewed:
            issues.append("BLOCKED: Legal review not completed")
        if not self.comms_reviewed:
            issues.append("BLOCKED: Comms review not completed")
        internal_detail = ["model version", "prompt", "context window", "eval suite"]
        combined = (self.what_clients_experienced + self.process_change).lower()
        for phrase in internal_detail:
            if phrase in combined:
                issues.append(f"External statement contains internal operational detail: {phrase!r}")
        return issues


stmt = ExternalIncidentStatement(
    organisation_name="Meridian Legal Partners",
    incident_date=date(2026, 7, 1),
    what_clients_experienced=(
        "A small number of contract summary documents contained an incorrect liability figure. "
        "The error affected documents generated on 1 July 2026 between 08:00 and 09:10 GMT. "
        "Our review team identified the error before documents were finalised and delivered. "
        "No client has received a document containing this error."
    ),
    immediate_remediation=(
        "All contracts processed during the affected window have been manually reviewed and "
        "verified. Clients whose matters were processed during this period will receive "
        "confirmation from their matter manager by end of business today."
    ),
    process_change=(
        "The automated processing step has been updated with additional validation checks. "
        "Analyst review procedures now require a specific check for liability figure accuracy "
        "on all contract summaries."
    ),
    contact_for_affected_clients="your.matterteam@meridian-legal.com or +44 20 7000 0000",
    statement_date=date(2026, 7, 1),
    legal_reviewed=True,
    comms_reviewed=True,
)

issues = stmt.pre_publish_check()
if issues:
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"  BLOCKED: {issue}")
else:
    print("Pre-publish check passed")
    print()
    print(stmt.render())
Post-Incident Learning and Communication Closure

The crisis communications cycle does not end when the immediate issue is resolved. The post-incident communication, sent to all employees who received the initial notice, completes the loop and is the most important signal of whether the organisation is genuinely learning or simply managing perception.
Post-Incident Communication Requirements
Root cause confirmed and specific.
Not 'the AI made an error' but the specific mechanism: which process decision allowed which failure condition to exist.
Named process changes.
The concrete changes made to the process, validation controls, or training content, with effective date.
Scope confirmed.
How many outputs were affected, whether any reached external parties, what remediation was provided.
Acknowledgement of what is not changed.
If the tool is being reinstated with the fix applied, say so. If it is being retired, say so. Silence about reinstatement leaves employees uncertain about whether to use the tool.
Named owner of the fix.
The person responsible for verifying that the process change has been implemented. Not a team.
Maintaining Adoption Trust Through Incidents

The counterintuitive finding from organisations that have managed AI incidents well is that a well-handled incident can increase trust in the programme more than a programme that has had no visible incidents at all. A programme that transparently identifies and fixes a failure demonstrates that the human oversight layer is working, that the organisation takes quality seriously, and that the error reporting system functions. A programme with no visible incidents may simply have a broken reporting culture.
Publish post-incident summaries in a searchable internal location. Employees can see the incident history, root causes, and fixes, which builds confidence that issues are not being hidden.
Measure incident report rates as a positive signal. A rising P3 near-miss rate in the first 6 months of a programme is good news: analysts are using the review process and reporting what they find.

Acknowledge limitations in safe-use guidance explicitly, not just in post-mortems. 'This tool has known limitations with X; here is how to handle them' prevents the failure mode where an analyst encounters a known edge case without knowing it is a known edge case.
Common Crisis Comms Failures
Failure
Why It Happens
Consequence
Fix
Attributing failure to 'the AI' with no process explanation
Technical teams describe the model behaviour; no one translates to process
Employees hear 'AI is unreliable'; adoption stalls
Require every root cause to name the human decision or process gap alongside the technical mechanism
Initial notice sent after the deadline
No pre-defined escalation path; incident owner unclear
Leadership appears unaware; rumour fills the gap
Playbook names the incident owner for each severity before any incident occurs
External statement before legal review
Comms pressure overrides review process
Statement creates additional liability or misrepresents facts
External statement is gated in the incident management system; cannot be published without legal sign-off
Post-incident communication skipped
Programme moves on after containment
Employees never learn what changed; trust gap remains
Post-incident comms is a mandatory closure step; incident cannot be marked closed without it
Incident log kept private from future teams
Treated as sensitive internal failure data
New teams repeat known failure modes
Post-incident summaries published to internal knowledge base after anonymisation
Summary

Crisis communications for AI incidents requires a pre-built playbook with named owners, time-bound response obligations, and approved message templates. Incidents are classified P1 (external harm), P2 (internal failure), or P3 (near-miss), with each level triggering a different response speed and audience. The initial internal notice confirms facts and immediate actions without speculating on root cause; the full root cause communication identifies the specific process gap and the concrete fix. For P1 incidents, external statements require legal review and avoid operational detail. The post-incident communication closes the loop and is the most important signal of whether the programme genuinely learns. Programmes that handle incidents transparently, publishing post-mortems and measuring near-miss reporting as a positive signal, consistently build more durable adoption trust than programmes with no visible incident history.
